sound_type: low thump + ratchet -- repetitive mechanical thump trains, zipper-like ratchet sounds, 'boing,' low-frequency downsweeps. Unlike any other cetacean. E=4
ecosystem: Open sea, continental shelf. Summer visitor to Washington waters. San Juan Islands. Circumpolar.
season: Summer (June-September) in Washington waters. Year-round in temperate/cold seas globally.

coordinates.description: Minke Whale in San Juan Islands / Strait of Juan de Fuca. Summer visitor. Open sea feeder.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST BALAENOPTERIDAE -- rorqual whale family enters. Smallest rorqual (7-10 m). Baleen filter feeder. Ventral pleats for lunge feeding. Fourth cetacean family. 11 consecutive marine mammals (180-190). Toothed-to-baleen transition.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Balaenoptera from Greek 'balaena' (whale) + 'pteron' (fin/wing). acutorostrata from Latin 'acutus' (sharp) + 'rostratus' (beaked) -- pointed snout. 'Minke' possibly from a Norwegian whaler named Meincke.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Balaenoptera -- 8 species. Largest genus of baleen whales. Includes blue, fin, sei, Bryde's, humpback, and minke whales.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Cetacea. Fourth cetacean family: Phocoenidae (186-187), Delphinidae (188-189), Balaenopteridae (190).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sixth marine mammal family: Phocidae (180-181), Otariidae (182-184), Mustelidae (185), Phocoenidae (186-187), Delphinidae (188-189), Balaenopteridae (190). 11 consecutive marine mammals.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Class Mammalia. Marine mammal era: 11 consecutive (180-190). Toothed-to-baleen transition. 6 families.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 80-5,000 Hz. Low to mid frequency. Infrasonic to audible.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Thumps: 100-500 Hz. Ratchet: 500-5,000 Hz. Downsweeps: 80-200 Hz. Boing: 1-4 kHz.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Thumps: 0.1-0.5 sec each, trains last minutes to hours. Ratchet: 0.5-3 sec.
song_metrics.note_structure: Thump pulses (rhythmic, regular), ratchet sequences (pulsed, mechanical), downsweeps (long-range), boing (distinctive).
song_metrics.note_types: Thump trains (communication), ratchet (social/feeding), downsweeps (long-range contact), boing (unknown function).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Repetitive thump trains dominate. Ratchet and boing interspersed. Not melodic -- mechanical and rhythmic.
song_metrics.timbre: Thumps: deep, percussive. Ratchet: mechanical, zipper-like. Boing: pulsed, distinctive. Downsweeps: low, powerful.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Distinctive but moderate by whale standards. Mechanical quality unique among cetaceans.
song_metrics.tempo: Thump trains: regular, rhythmic, 1-3 second intervals. Can persist for hours.
song_metrics.context: Open sea, continental shelf. Summer feeding. Solitary or pairs. Long-range communication.
song_metrics.regional_variation: North Pacific 'boing' distinct from other populations. Regional vocalization differences documented.
song_metrics.key_property: LOW THUMP + RATCHET. FIRST BALAENOPTERIDAE. Smallest rorqual. Baleen filter feeder. Mechanical sounds. 11 consecutive marine mammals. E=4. v1.49.331.

pelage.overall: Dark gray to black dorsally, white ventrally. Distinctive white band on pectoral flippers. Pointed, V-shaped head. Single prominent dorsal fin at two-thirds body length. 50-70 ventral pleats.
pelage.diagnostic_mark: White pectoral flipper band. Pointed head. Dorsal fin prominent. Small by whale standards but still 7-10 m.
pelage.swimming_pattern: Fast swimmer for a baleen whale (up to 30 km/h). Can breach (leap clear of water) but less demonstrative than humpbacks. Low, inconspicuous blow.

physical_description.overall: Length: 7-10 m. Weight: 5,000-10,000 kg. Smallest rorqual. Streamlined body. Pointed head. 230-360 baleen plates per side. 50-70 ventral pleats.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White pectoral flipper band diagnostic. Pointed head. Dorsal fin appears simultaneously with back when surfacing.
physical_description.field_id: Small whale (by whale standards) with pointed head and white flipper band. Low, inconspicuous blow. Often solitary. Fast swimmer. Summer visitor to inland waters.

nodes.title: First Balaenopteridae -- Rorquals Enter
nodes.description: First rorqual whale. Fourth cetacean family. Baleen replaces teeth. Ventral pleats enable lunge feeding. The family of the largest animals on Earth.

nodes.title: Thump + Ratchet -- Mechanical Whale Music
nodes.description: Thump trains: rhythmic, mechanical pulses. Ratchet: zipper-like pulsed sounds. The 'boing': initially misidentified as non-biological. Unique among cetaceans.

nodes.title: 11 Consecutive Marine Mammals
nodes.description: Degrees 180-190. 6 families. Toothed-to-baleen transition at 190. The record extends into unprecedented territory.

cross_references.relationship: TOOTHED TO BALEEN! Last toothed cetacean to first baleen whale. Fundamental feeding strategy transition.
CETACEAN DIVERSITY! Three families in three degrees: Delphinidae (188-189), Balaenopteridae (190). Rapid expansion.
THE UNDERSTATED! Harvey Danger = one-hit wonder with depth. Minke = smallest rorqual with structural significance. Total E=9.
